item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The figure in white is owned by Getty Images, by Erik Von Weber. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Votes don't help the author, so I've vowed to always leave a comment with mine, at the very least whenever I rate an article negatively. If you feel the same, help out by leaving critique with more of your votes. Comment follows:

The premise of this is excellent, setting up some wonderful implications, but then it attempts to execute those implications and falls on its face. I wish the description had stopped just before the paragraph starting "Within 5 days of SCP-2816-2's first change," - before that, you have this foreboding sense that something bad is going to happen and hazmat dude has a mysterious mission, but after that point the cloth is pulled away and the answer is exposed. There's something lost when the mystery is revealed. Once the point is made, the test logs make the same point again and again without any development. It's a shame - there's an excellent build up, but the rest of the article lets it decay. -1

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image source is here, and is in the public domain, therefore it is CC complaint with the wiki's license.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't know about that! That's really interesting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great SCP!

One question: was this in any way a reference to the real-life method of determining forged paintings by nuclear fallout?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the thousand or so skips I have read over the past decade or so, I've never come across such a thing. Mega bonus points for originality, and just overall good writing combined with some excellent test logs!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang, this was great! Fantastic use of the ethos of those paintings to deliver a haunting little story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really fantastic. If anything, I wanted to see more testing logs (something tells me all is not well in American Gothic). Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Literally the best art-based skip on the site (in my own opinion). I am in awe.

Wonderful work, sir, wonderful work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one. Leaves just the right amount of questions unanswered. This is a good example of an SCP that leaves you thinking about it long after you've finished reading it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I for one welcome our new bartending overlord. Laphroaig on the rocks, please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
C'mon guys, be nice! You should be SOOOOOOO pleased to have a brand-new good SCP on our beautiful site written by a very promising writer!

(Yeah. Me too. I envy all your successful skips that didn't get shot down at -29 in less than a day.)
<VBG>

+114. Well done, PYP! ;-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having, like, the worst case of "Successful first skip is better than my first" envy right now.

Hearing you loud and clear, Agent MacLeod.

+1 given with a cold, bitter, grudging heart.

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Ahaha I have been hearing that from a number of people, I'm honestly just so blown away with how well this did. I really couldn't have done it without the help of the community who gave me so much great feedback and critique so you can thank everyone who did that too! (which I think includes you as well, haha)

The guy/gal/other entity that hit this to +100 didn't post, so let me go ahead and rectify that. Great job, PYP! I'm having, like, the worst case of "Successful first skip is better than my first" envy right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh gosh, you're totally right! I didn't even notice that. I couldn't stop looking at it once you pointed it out so I went ahead and fixed it, thank you very much for pointing it out! :D

And thank you so much for the kind words, I'm very glad you enjoyed the article! ;w;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused by the whole thing, but in a good way. Like, who is this guy? Why does he do what he does? What in the world is going on here? But the article doesn't need those questions answered - it works exactly as it is, and does everything it needs to do, no more, no less.
Also, your image editing skills are superb. One thing about the image: The stool the hazmat guy is sitting on does not have a shaded top like the stool that the native painting-dweller is sitting on. Barely noticeable though, still great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK. Not so sure about it, but I still believe it should be made clearer in the containment procedures…! ;>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The CCTV cameras are there to keep the paintings in a static state until testing is desired, although that knowledge isn't really something anyone retrieving them needs to know. For containing instances of SCP-2816-1, it's sufficient to be told to put them in front of a CCTV camera.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You want to have controlled periods of non-observation for testing. You want to keep them static for storage. The whole experimental process is about controlled variables.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, what I got from the article was that the paintings only change when they are not observed, and changes begin as soon as the forgery is finished using the paintbrushes.

In that sense, you kind of need to put the paintings in storage to test them.

But yeah, if you want to stall the testing process, using a CCTV camera would work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would they want the paintings changing in storage? Wouldn't they want to preserve the paintings for testing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The paintings that SCP-2816-2 appear in are forgeries (aka copies) of the original work. Since they are copies and inherently without value, why would the foundation add CCTV cameras, especially when the interest is in studying 2816's effects.

:I
Congratulations dude!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Four weeks and about twelve major revisions. Wow!

Excellent job. Both creepy and poetic, I think. SCP-2816-2 is ruining famous paintings… in order to save the life of the painted humans. I love it.

Just one nitpick: I feel the containment procedures are incomplete. Shouldn't you add that the CCTV cameras are there to occasionally prevent a painting from being changed? Pretty obvious of course once you read the whole SCP, but containment procedures are supposed to be clear and accurate, are they not?

Have your +1, my friend, with a beautiful ribbon artistically tied around it (I let you choose the colour)!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahaha thank you very much!!! I was really pleased with how the image turned out, and it makes me really happy every time someone notices the work I put into it :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It looks really good. If I could upvote images separately from the article, I would. Alas you only get the one upvote for both.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I spent an afternoon photoshopping this photo into an image of the painting, then adding various filters and manually adjusting the edges until it looked like it fit the art style and lighting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, that was mostly memory, my art history has never been good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where does the modified "Nighthawks" come from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2816-2 reset all clocks to 12 o'clock

That reference to the doomsday clock.

In all seriousness, this is an astoundingly good SCP. Your persistance for improvement and willingness to adapt is shown, and the result is a SCP that is a work of art. (Ohoho, pun.)
You sir, have earnt all the +1's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole time I read this, I was thinking, "Don't we already have something similar?" In concept, maybe, but the effects stand on their own. I dearly love what you did with Guernica at the end. And it's nice to see something that makes an anomalous painting that isn't just "ooh, spooky creepman haunts yer paintin's" for once. Well done.

item.title: Re: SCPNews
item.content:encoded: SCPNews should definitely be made into a thing.

item.title: Re: SCPNews
item.content:encoded: Well congrats man! I'm Amuness Creeps with SCPNews er some shite

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I asked a friend of mine who is well-versed in the effects of nuclear detonations for some advice on how a nuke might affect the ISS (which I decided made for a good test subject), and here's what he said:

Probably not anything visually interesting. It's basically a lot of very hard X-rays. The best source I know of is Nukes In Space. But it's not going to be visually interesting like it would be on the ground.

If we rule out a hard kill, the most likely effect is the nuke pumping up the radiation background and damaging electronics and crew that way. I don't know how good the EMP shielding on the ISS is, so that's another possibility. (My guess is low, because it's international and stuff.)

The direct effects would be impulsive shock (if it was close enough), and lots of x-rays (if it wasn't). The first is going to shred the thing. The second isn't going to do a lot to the outside. What would be interesting is if you painted the inside.

item.title: Re: SCPNews
item.content:encoded:
FASDFJASDOI I DIDN'T EXPECT PEOPLE TO LIKE IT THIS MUCH I'M SO PROUD

Uhhhhhh *cough* according to my folder, four weeks and about 12 major revisions.

item.title: SCPNews
item.content:encoded:
Pick Yer Poison, with SCPNews and people want to know how you feel about your first SCP becoming a grand success? How long did it take you to make this SCP?

*hands microphone*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done indeed. +1.

item.title: Good work.
item.content:encoded: A short but interesting article, with an idea that can really be explored. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very happy to finally see this posted. Excellent job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much! I've incorporated those changes. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. The photo is a nice touch to set the mood of the article. I don't have a whole lot to say beyond what others have mentioned, beyond the fact that I'm glad this isn't weighed down by additional supplements and other documentation when that very easily could have been what happened. It's as long as it needs to be, and does what it's supposed to. Pretty solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Head's up:

  • Geiger is a proper noun. It's a name. Capitalize it.
  • HAZMAT is usually (though not always) spelled in All-Caps. Furthermore, there are multiple forms of HAZMAT protective gear; in the US, they use an alphabetized system. Level C is a suit you're taped into and wear a gas mask (which means a mask with a filter) with. Level B is something you're taped into and wear an SCBA mask with; you can have an air supply hose or a tank on your back, but no air filter. Level A is a spacesuit; you're zippered in, with your tank inside the suit with you, protecting you from all but the gods themselves. If this fella is wearing a commercially-available one, it's probably Type C, or just a painter's basic PPE with a gas mask added in for good measure.

Otherwise, solidly within my spectrum for cool/creepy/weird. I wanna know what he'd do if he met SCP-085. Someone should write that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As others have said, great article. Simple, but works just perfectly. I also liked seeing your many different ideas in chat (some of which I reviewed drafts of) and admire your work on this object. It definitely paid off, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you both! I changed "window" to "opening" for accuracy. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its not often that someone pings me in chat, let alone with a request to read someone else's article. However, in this case I'm glad that they did.

This was a wonderful article. Neat premise, short enough that id didn't drag, while long enough to build a great atmosphere. All in all this is everything I look for in an article. I think that this one is easily going to be in my top 10.

10/10 Pick Yer Poison.
Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
She is sitting in front of a loggia on an upper story. I guess you can argue that a loggia is somehow not a window, but is that correct? It certainly isn't an entrance. It's meant to be an opening to the air for a sitting gallery.

You can board it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Mona Lisa does not have a window in the back around.

Other than that, a beautiful SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I…that's a good question.

Whatever it is, it would probably be very confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. Would be nice to see a suplementar test log with more paintings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna know what would happen to an Escher painting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, probably whatever effects would occur if you set off a nuclear device in space, rather than if you set it off on Earth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect the closest thing, like a supernova for the space example.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, good catch. I'll change it to reflect that, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if they reproduce a painting in a setting where a nuclear winter is impossible (i.e. a painting of space?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice touch with Guernica at the end. No one left for him to save there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The brushes emit minor amounts of radiation

I suggest changing that to ionizing radiation to be more specific, since you later say that they get radiation poisoning if the nuclear winter hits them. Other than that, I really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
, but any attempt to copy an existing painting will make the result an instance of SCP-2816-1.

You should make it clear that the anomalous effect only takes place after the painting is finished.

I remember seeing this on site19 a week ago, and i'm loving the changes you made. The version I saw lacked the direction this one has.
